rationales.achievement: They "mapped out the first unit already" and wanted to "set this up right from the start," showing deliberate effort to do the job well.
rationales.benevolence: They spent their own money on worksheets because otherwise "the kids lose momentum," centering the students' needs over their own convenience.

rationales.self_direction: They turned down the job with "full benefits" because "the literacy program here is the whole reason I came," sticking with a path they chose for themselves.
rationales.security: They may "wish I'd just taken the safe thing" but still plan not to call back, choosing against a more stable option.
rationales.benevolence: "I keep thinking about Mateo and the other kids who just started trusting me" shows the decision was driven by care for the children relying on them.

rationales.self_direction: "I still can't make myself call her back" shows they are holding to their own chosen direction even after replaying the decision all night.
rationales.security: They acknowledge the other salary would have covered "student loans and left room to breathe" yet continue refusing the safer financial path.

rationales.self_direction: They deleted the county job emails because picturing "a curriculum someone else wrote" was something "in me just won't do," rejecting a more controlled path.
rationales.security: With "possible mid-year budget cuts" and safer jobs offering "steady pay" and a "pension," deleting those options goes against stability.

rationales.benevolence: They call Mateo reading to his little brother "the whole point," showing the day mattered because of what it did for a student's home life.

rationales.self_direction: They describe the good exhaustion as coming from having "actually built" something, unlike "following someone else's plan all day."
rationales.achievement: "Two kids moved from level B to level D in three weeks" and they reorganized the baskets so each child can find the right book, emphasizing concrete progress and competence.
rationales.benevolence: They stayed late so "every kid can find a book at their level," putting students' learning needs at the center.

rationales.self_direction: "Money isn't the only thing that matters" and being "tired of everyone acting like it should be" shows they are choosing by their own priorities rather than others' standards.
rationales.security: After calculating an extra "nine hundred a month," they still dismiss money as the deciding factor, moving away from the safer financial option.

rationales.self_direction: "I don't want to build my life around avoiding the worst-case scenario" and "I want to build it around something I actually chose" is a direct assertion of self-authored choice.
rationales.security: They admit "the safer path is right there" with better benefits and a pension, but refuse it anyway.
rationales.benevolence: "I chose the kids" shows the decision is anchored in care for the students rather than personal ease.

rationales.self_direction: "I keep choosing this instead" and "right now I'm staying" shows a continued commitment to the path they personally want.
rationales.security: Even after weeks of worry and with the "safer move" easy to list on paper, they continue rejecting the more stable option.
They say supporting the motion would put long-standing client ties at risk "financially" and "personally," so preserving those stable relationships drives the choice.
rationales.conformity: They "didn't say anything" in the meeting because "you don't blow that up in a board meeting," prioritizing smooth relations over open conflict.
rationales.universalism: Despite recent photos of workers without proper protective gear, they stayed silent and let the motion fail rather than back broader protection for vulnerable people.

rationales.security: They funded the delayed pump installation immediately even though their wife said to wait until "cash flow is better," choosing action over caution.
rationales.benevolence: The son's call mattered enough that they acted right away, and "I told him I was proud" shows support for someone close to them shaped the decision.
rationales.universalism: They transferred money to cover a "clean-water pump installation" and said "some things shouldn't wait," prioritizing help for people beyond their immediate circle.

rationales.conformity: They could have asked the hard questions but stayed quiet because raising it would mean "delaying the whole partnership review," going along with the existing course.
rationales.universalism: They suspected the solvent was unsafe, "could smell it from the parking lot," and still chose not to ask about possible harm to workers.

rationales.benevolence: They frame the business as what funds "my children's projects" and other giving, showing responsibility toward people in their orbit.
rationales.universalism: "The ability to actually do something about the problems I see" and funding "the foundation" and "scholarships" shows a broad, outward-looking sense of responsibility.

rationales.conformity: Saying "the agenda is always packed and nobody wants to hear philosophy from the recycling guy" shows reluctance to disrupt the room's expectations.
rationales.universalism: They connect the hawker uncle's lease worries to "small people squeezed by systems that don't see them," treating a wider social pattern as something that deserves action.

rationales.conformity: They "didn't push back" and "changed the subject," choosing table harmony over challenging the group.
rationales.universalism: They let comments dismissing migrant worker housing pass without objection, even though the issue "sat with me on the drive home," leaving broader harm unchallenged.
They chose the cheaper route even though the company "cuts corners on packaging for rural deliveries" and someone may face "a damaged pump at the other end."
